questions.question: What is a managed agent?
options: An agent loop that runs entirely on Anthropic's infrastructure
A tool that manually orchestrates Claude API calls on your own servers
A pre-built agent template that cannot be customized
A service that only handles file operations and nothing else
questions.explanation: A managed agent is an agent loop that runs on Anthropic's infrastructure rather than your own servers. This removes the need to manage while loops, tool orchestration, session persistence, and execution environments yourself. The other options misrepresent either the location of execution or the flexibility of managed agents.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four core primitives of managed agents?
options: Agent
Environment
Session
Model Registry
questions.explanation: The four core primitives are Agent, Environment, Session, and Events. A Model Registry is not part of the managed agent framework. The Agent defines the model, system prompt, toolset, and capabilities. The Environment specifies where the agent runs. The Session represents a single execution. Events are the communication layer.

questions.question: What does the `session.status_idle` event indicate?
options: The agent is waiting for user input
The session has completed its work and is ready to terminate
The environment is temporarily unavailable
A tool execution has failed and the agent is retrying
questions.explanation: The `session.status_idle` event signals that the session has completed its work. This is the termination signal for the workflow and indicates that the agent is done processing. It is not a signal for waiting input, environmental issues, or tool failures.

questions.question: Why is it important to open the event stream before sending the initial user request?
options: The stream requires authentication that only works before sending messages
The stream only receives events that occur after it has been established
Opening the stream first reduces latency by pre-warming the connection
The API will reject any messages sent without an open stream, regardless of timing
questions.explanation: The event stream only receives events that occur after it has been established. If you send a message before opening the stream, you will miss the events generated by that message. This is a critical ordering requirement in the managed agent workflow, not an authentication or performance optimization issue.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between an Agent and a Session in the managed agent model?
options: An Agent is a reusable definition, while a Session represents a single execution of that Agent
An Agent runs on Anthropic's infrastructure, while a Session runs on your own servers
An Agent can only be used once, while a Session can be reused across multiple requests
An Agent contains the event stream, while a Session contains the environment configuration
questions.explanation: An Agent is a reusable definition that contains the model, system prompt, toolset, and capabilities. A Session is a single execution of an agent inside a specific environment, representing one unit of work. Multiple sessions can reuse the same agent definition. The other options incorrectly describe the infrastructure, reusability, or component relationships.

questions.question: What role does the Environment play in a managed agent workflow?
options: It stores the agent's system prompt and behavioral instructions
It defines where the agent runs and provides containerized execution, file system access, and networking configuration
It manages the communication between the application and the agent
It determines which Claude model the agent will use
questions.explanation: The Environment defines where the agent runs and provides the sandbox in which work occurs. It includes containerized execution, file system access, networking configuration, and runtime isolation. The system prompt is part of the Agent definition, communication happens through Events, and the model is specified in the Agent.

questions.question: In the managed agent architecture, what is the purpose of Events?
options: To store the agent's conversation history and session state
To serve as the communication layer for all activity including user requests, tool usage, agent responses, and status updates
To define the permissions and access controls for the agent
To monitor and log performance metrics of the agent execution
questions.explanation: Events are the communication layer through which everything flows: user requests, tool usage, agent responses, and status updates. Everything enters and exits the system through events. Events are not for storage, permissions, or monitoring—they are the primary mechanism for interaction with the managed agent.

questions.question: Which scenario is most suitable for using a managed agent instead of a manual agent loop?
options: A lightweight request that needs to call Claude once and return immediately
A workflow that requires fine-grained control over each step of the agent loop
A long-running task that touches many files, requires persistence, and must survive interruptions
An application where you need to minimize the number of API calls to Claude
questions.explanation: Managed agents are ideal for long-running, stateful workflows that require file access, persistence, recovery from interruptions, and continuous progress updates. Lightweight single-call requests, scenarios requiring fine-grained control, and API call minimization are better served by manual loops or direct API calls. Managed agents excel when operational complexity and runtime duration make self-managed infrastructure expensive.

questions.question: What does the `agent.tool_use` event represent?
options: A request from the user asking the agent to use a specific tool
An indication that the agent has selected and executed a tool
A notification that a tool has failed and the agent is retrying
A configuration message that enables or disables tools in the environment
questions.explanation: The `agent.tool_use` event indicates that the agent has selected and executed a tool. It is not a user request, a failure notification, or a configuration message. This event allows you to track and respond to tool activity as it happens during the agent's execution.

questions.question: Consider a scenario where you need to build an agent that processes thousands of files, archives duplicates, and flags uncertain cases over several minutes. What architectural advantages does a managed agent provide over a manual loop in this context?
options: Managed agents can process files faster because they use a different Claude model
Managed agents eliminate the need to manage infrastructure, handle state persistence, and implement custom resilience logic, while providing built-in resumability and continuous event streaming
Managed agents allow you to avoid using the file system entirely by storing all files in memory
Managed agents automatically optimize file operations by compressing data before processing
questions.explanation: For long-running file-processing workflows, managed agents provide significant operational advantages: Anthropic handles the agent loop execution, state persistence, resumability, and infrastructure management. You focus on agent configuration and event consumption. Manual loops would require you to implement custom retry logic, state management, and infrastructure provisioning. The other options misrepresent the capabilities of managed agents.

questions.question: In the managed agent workflow, what is the relationship between the order of operations and event visibility?
options: Events are buffered and delivered in batches after the session completes, so order does not matter
The event stream must be opened before sending the kickoff message because the stream only receives events that occur after it is established, making order critical for capturing all activity
Events are stored in the Agent definition and can be retrieved at any time, regardless of when the stream is opened
The order of operations is flexible because the Environment automatically replays missed events when the stream connects
questions.explanation: The event stream only receives events that occur after it has been established. This makes the order of operations critical: you must open the stream before sending the initial user request, or you will miss the events generated by that request. Events are not buffered for later delivery, stored in the Agent, or automatically replayed. Understanding this ordering requirement is essential for correctly implementing managed agent workflows.

questions.question: How do the responsibilities differ between you and Anthropic when using managed agents versus manual loops?
options: With managed agents, you manage the loop and infrastructure; with manual loops, Anthropic manages everything

With managed agents, Anthropic manages loop execution, sandboxes, state handling, and infrastructure while you focus on agent configuration and event consumption; with manual loops, you manage all of these aspects
With managed agents, you have no control over the agent's behavior; with manual loops, you have complete control
With managed agents, Anthropic handles event consumption; with manual loops, you handle event consumption
questions.explanation: The responsibility split is fundamental to understanding when to use each approach. With managed agents, Anthropic handles the operational burden (loop execution, sandboxes, state, infrastructure, resumability), while you focus on configuration and integration. With manual loops, you own all of these responsibilities. This shift in responsibility is why managed agents reduce operational overhead for long-running, complex workflows. The other options misrepresent the control and responsibility model.
